speaker: interviewer
text: Date-- June 9, 1995. Survivor-- Nathan Peters-- Last name at birth, Podeszwa. The interviewer is Gila Reinstein. We are in Roslyn, New York, in the United States, and the interview will be in English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is Gila Reinstein. Today is June 9, 1995. I'm interviewing Nathan Peters in Roslyn, New York, for the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation Project. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Would you please state and spell your name as it is now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Nathan Peters. N-A-T-H-A--

speaker: interviewer
text: N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name, Peters.

speaker: interviewee
text: P-E-T-E-R-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was [? Menachem ?] Podeszwa, P-O-D-E-S-Z-W-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you known by any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell us what your birthday is?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is December 20, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today is?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born at shtetl in the Ukraine by the name of Austrazetz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-U-S-T-R-A-Z-E-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's start by talking a bit about life before the war and what it was like for you in Austrazetz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why don't we start by you describing who's in your family, your immediate family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the shtetl that-- that I was born, this was a very small place, approximately between 600 and 800 people. It was a strictly Jewish shtetl. My-- my mother had-- was one of seven sisters. One sister was in America before the war, and six of them lived in the same shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name was Kessleman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: K-E-S-S-L-E-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Israel Podeszwa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was sometimes known as--

speaker: interviewee
text: Srul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or Srulek.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Austrazetz was part of what country when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- before 1939, it was part of the Polish Ukraine. And in 1939, when the Germans invaded Poland, it-- it was under-- it became under Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the nearest big town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The nearest big town was Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it-- was there another city that was even bigger that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz-- Lodz was the closest one, about 16 kilometers. Then as they added, a bigger city was Rowno, R-O-W-N-O, I believe. And that was already a city of about 25,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the-- before '39, we had-- we had a store, which he sold-- everything you can name it, he sold it. He sold cloth for-- for suits, and he sold herring. Whatever you want, he sold in that store. And on top of that, he used to be a peddler, take stuff out to the farmers because they couldn't come into the city to buy. So they used to go out to them and sell to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other stores in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there were probably another three, four similar stores in the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your brother and sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- I-- I have one-- one brother and two sisters. I have an older brother, which was five years older than I am, which is already deceased right now. I have a sister, who is three years older-- older than I am, who lives in Los Angeles. And-- and I had a small sister by the name of Chayka, who was seven months old. When we left the ghetto, she was left with my grandmother, and they were-- and they were both killed in the-- in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In your home life before the war, could you describe what your house was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In comparison to living in Europe, now, the way I understand it now, it was pretty well off because my father had a-- had a store, and a peddler. And we always had food on the table. And I had 13 cousins to-- to play with. So life as we understood at the time was very good-- was very good for me. Then when the-- when the Russians came in, they-- they took away the stores and took away everybody that had to do with business. They took everything away. Life became a little harder. But again, as a kid, I went to school. And it was very normal as far as I was concerned-- a kid of 10 years old, 11 years old at that time already. It was-- it was a normal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things did you do for fun with your cousins and friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we-- we-- we'd go down to the-- to the water, which is not-- not far away. And we all-- we all had a small shtetl. We all had either a cow, and horses. And we used to, you know, take-- take them out to the fields to-- to graze them. And normal child play, really. Nothing-- we didn't have Disneyland, but it was very good for us at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What water was that-- a river or a lake?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a real small lake, a small lake. It was not-- it was not a river-- it was a small lake.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, living-- living in a-- in a-- in a shtetl, so we had some restrictions. Initially, I went to a Polish school, you know, elementary school. And because we were three kids school age, I had to wait one year before I can start. So I started a year later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it-- it was so-- the school was so small that if you had three kids at the same time-- and the older one was on his way out with finishing, so I had to wait a year before I started my grade. They just put me a year back. But I-- I only went to school, like, two and a half years. Then the war-- the war started. And then-- then they change it from a Polish school to a-- to a Russian school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the studies change when the government changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't. It didn't. I mean, it was very early in elementary school, so the studies didn't change that much, except for the language. It went from Polish language to Russian language. It was not that much difficult to do, because the alphabet is similar. So it was not a very difficult a change at this stage-- you know, when you were-- at that time, I was in third grade, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the language at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, it was strictly Yiddish. We spoke Yiddish, because my grandmother lived with us. So we spoke 100% Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did everyone in the family also speak Polish and Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, yes. Except for my grandmother-- I'm sure she did not speak a lot of Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandmother's name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Bayla.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kessleman, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When your brother finished school and you started, what did your brother-- what did Aaron do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was-- he was-- you know, he only finished a couple years of college, which is very unusual for-- for a Jewish guy. He went to Rowno for college, but when the war started, it all really stopped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister-- your older sister was Fanny.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fanny, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I believe you told me that she married?

speaker: interviewee
text: She married over here, and she lives in LA.

speaker: interviewer
text: She married in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: This country. She married in 1954 or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her married name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gradow. Gradow, G-R-A-D-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Getting back to-- to life in the Ukraine, what kind of Jewish life did you have in your family, in terms of observance, in terms of Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we went to cheder. You know, cheder, that was the part of Jewish education. You went to cheder before you went to-- to grammar school, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you started?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I started cheder, I probably was five or six years old. So I went to cheder before I went to grammar school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you started the grammar school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was seven. I was seven, because they usually start  at six, but I had to wait a year, so-- so I was seven years old. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the-- what the cheder looked like, what it was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cheder-- the cheder was conducted in the shul. And we had a rabbi as-- not a rabbi, really a melamed, that we used to call it. She was really a teacher. And most-- most of-- most of them were boys. Most of the kids that went to cheder were boys. Really, the girls, didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I remember. It wasn't an official class, but I don't remember any in my-- and after school-- at that time, for me, was before school. But even when I went to school, after school, you went to cheder. And at cheder, you-- the melamed, which was already a very-- probably in his 60s, or maybe older. And all we really learned is to [INAUDIBLE] and Russia-- and-- and not Russian, but from the-- from the [INAUDIBLE]. You know, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not Russian languages--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rashi, the Torah commentator?

speaker: interviewee
text: Torah commentator. And we-- mostly it was [INAUDIBLE] and the five books of Moses. That's as far, really, that I remember that I went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: For children today going to Hebrew school after public school, they're tired and it's hard. Was it like that for cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't take-- no, it was for something to do. You know, when we have-- I think our grammar school at that time-- if I remember, it was only like four hours or five hours a day. And we did not resent going to cheder. It was like a given. After school, you go straight to cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many children were in your class in grammar school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, now, it's-- I really don't exactly-- I would say probably in the 26-- between 20 and 26, depends on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the same group of-- and then all those boys, they went to the cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them went to cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the level of observance in your family, in terms of Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we observed kashrut. We had a kosher home. We went to-- we went to shul Friday and Saturday. And we had--- after shul, we had over-- over some-- kiddish, we used to call. They used to-- used to come home from shul. But they were-- my immediate family was observant, but not-- not orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that typical in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were a lot of orthodox people. We had two different shuls. One of them was very, very orthodox, you know. You-- you dressed in the black, and you had the [INAUDIBLE]. And it was two different cheders-- I mean, depends which one you went. And there were very-- some of them were very observant Jews. I mean, nobody rode on a Saturday. Or-- or we didn't have no cars. We didn't have to worry about that. But even your horse and buggy-- nobody rode on the Sabbath. It was an observant, really Yiddish shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no cars at all in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first car I think I saw was-- was in 1940, I believe. I mean-- that-- it was we didn't have enough-- we didn't have any asphalt to go. You probably had to go five kilometers on mud before you went to the highway to go to Lodz. So it was-- cars-- cars were not-- through our town, till the Russians came in and-- and they build a road that connected to go to Lodz. But till then, we really-- we didn't see. The first cars we saw was the-- first was the Russian army and then was the German army coming on motorcycles was probably the first cars we've ever seen in our shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not have electricity till either '40 or '41, which is before. Under-- when we-- when we won the Polish domination, we did not have electricity. But when the Russian came in, they did put in electricity.

speaker: interviewer
text: The year the Russians came, that was-- what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then there were-- there was some modernization and some changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some changes, yeah. And but they did-- they did liquidate all the-- all businesses. And a few people that they knew, they were, like, more politically involved or something, they picked them up at night, and they sent them to Siberia. There was not too many in our town. There was not too many.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the store was liquidated, what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the baron, you know, the one that-- that-- that had all the lands, what the Russian did, they-- they took all the land and divided it. And they gave each family a piece of it-- an acre, two acres, I really don't remember how many-- how much. We did-- we did get some, and we cultivated the land. And then also, we lived from the savings that my family had. And the stuff that they hid. They didn't let them liquidate. So they sold it on black market. It was survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you recall what the-- what it-- what you grew or-- or raised on the land?

speaker: interviewee
text: We grew corn, just, you know, wheat, to have for bread. And just normal-- and potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the baron?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think-- I really don't know what happened. Well, I know the minute the Russian came in, they picked him up, and he's in Siberia-- or they sent him to Siberia, and we never heard from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he-- did the Jewish community have a relationship with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very good to the Jewish community. To my knowledge, what I remember, he was very good. He especially was very good, not to my immediate family, but to my mother's sisters. He was very good to them because he created for them a place to live. They-- he took all the big barn and divided them and gave each one-- one had two rooms, one had one room, but they all had a roof. And I mean, he was the one that did it for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why he did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I think-- I believe-- which I really can wonder. One of my grandfathers, which I never knew-- they all passed away before I could remember-- they-- one of them had some relations with him. And I think it was my mother's father had relation. That's why he-- he took-- he gave them all that particular building.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many sisters did your mother have?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had six sisters. She was one of seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one was already in--?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was already in America before the war, and six of them all lived-- all lived in the same shtetl. And five of them lived under-- under the same roof, except us. We had-- we had our own house. And all of them were married, and each one-- if I remember exactly, they had like-- I had 13 cousins, and only two of them survived. And only one-- one of the six sisters which were in shtetl survived. All the-- all the-- all the others were-- did not-- did not survive. On my father's side, he was one of four. He had two sisters, and there were two brothers. One sister was still single. Now married. And his-- his brother, whose name was [? Sholem ?], he had-- he had three kids. And his-- and his-- and his sister, whose name was [? Malka, ?] she had two kids, but none-- none of them survived. From my father's family, none of them survived. He was one of four. None of them-- none of them survived. And my grandmother one of them that passed away while she was in ghetto. And the other one was liquidated while the ghetto was liquidated in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we'll get-- we'll get to that very soon. Was your father's family also living in Austrazetz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all of them were living in Austrazetz, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father did business as a peddler, he would go to the surrounding countryside. Were there Jews or non-Jews in the countryside that he would trade with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jews-- the-- the only Jew-- there was one family that I-- I can remember. There was non-Jews-- there were a lot of Ukraines and Czech. A lot of Czechoslovakian-- and they had big farms in our area at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you remember about your grandmother, Bayla, who lived with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- well, we had a store. While my-- while my father used to go into countryside, my mother used to be in the store, and also one of her sisters, the one that was survived. But they used-- they used to handle the store. So my grandmother always lived with us. Since the day I can remember, she always lived with us. So she, like, actually took care of us most of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she filled in when your mother was working in the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: She always lived with us, yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the first signs of danger, the first signs of threat to the Jewish community that you can remember in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1941, when the-- when the Germans invaded Russia, and it only took them, if I can remember exactly, probably like, 10 days to come in-- I mean, to-- to-- to overrun us. The first big danger-- it was a month or five weeks after they came in. And one day, they-- in the street, they picked up every able man that looked like a grown man, if he was 13 or 14 or 15. They picked him up, and at that time, they probably picked up between 80, and 90 men only. And they took them out four kilometers in a-- in a-- in a forest. They dug a grave, made them kneel, and they shot them in the back, and they all fell in. And the reason I'm-- I'm so much aware exactly how they were killed-- because there were a lot of rumors going on for months. They were working over here and working over there, because nobody wanted to believe that's what's going to happen. One of my distant relatives, distant uncle, he was one of them. And he fell in alive, and he came back. And all the rumors that were going on for weeks-- they were working over here and working over  there. We knew, but he was in hiding. He couldn't come out and say. We knew that where-- they are four kilometers in the forest, and they're all dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the-- the town people had been told that the men would be taken for work?

speaker: interviewee
text: To work. Taken to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it soldiers who came to take them? Nazi soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably-- about that time, it was probably about a half a dozen Germans, and the rest of them were Ukraine police, which worked with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they were cooperating, helping?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our area, where I was born, the Ukraines probably did 90% of the killing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when they liquidated our ghetto, which later on, I believe they had, like, three Germans. The rest of them were all-- all Ukraines. They just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, shortly after the Germans invaded, they rounded up this group of men. They took them out and murdered them. Your distant uncle came back. He had to hide then, because he was supposed to be dead and gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, because he's supposed to be dead. So he-- he nobody ever saw him, really. I mean, except my-- my father saw him. I never see him for a while. But-- but while we were in hiding, you know, the farther you tell the story. Everybody thought they're working over here and working over there, you know, all the rumors. But he knew they were four kilometers away, and none of them are  alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next-- next in our town, for a while it was like they used to pick everybody up to go to work. I mean, somehow they organized a-- a Judenrat, I think they called them. And they used to come in and say, well, we need so many people to go to work, and so many people-- and then had rations. And that probably lasted for about four or five months.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Judenrat was run by Jewish leadership?

speaker: interviewee
text: By Jewish leadership, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody in your family involved in the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was involved in that for a while, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what-- what his-- what his responsibilities might have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: His-- his responsibility was, you know, like to-- not-- not so much as being a policeman, there were [INAUDIBLE] but to-- to deal with the Germans. You know, we-- we had- they set up a-- an office, you know, like it was a lieutenant, and probably like, four, five German to deal with them, and whatever-- whatever came up, you know, whatever they wanted. So for him to deal with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you suppose he was given that-- that responsibility?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my-- my-- in our shtetl, my father was like, you know, the guy that-- he was a businessman. He was a-- I know that he can handle problems. And they had a lot of respect for him, because he used to socialize with the rabbis. So he was given status in-- in the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speaking of the rabbi for a moment, do you recall his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't recall his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what became of him or-- and his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he-- he perished in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right, so months are going by. There's a Judenrat established. Rationing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, food rationing-- you had to.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing on a daily basis? Were you still going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no school at that time. It was really no. First of all, at that-- at that time, which I was almost 11, they-- they even send us to work. I mean, picking-- picking apples, picking this here. They-- lined up every day, we went-- we went to work. And when you-- when you went to work, it was really a blessing at that time because somehow you always managed to steal something, you know, to-- to bring home. Because if you would sit in the ghetto, I mean, there was only bricks and-- and dirt. That-- that was it. But at least when you went to work, so you either stole apples or pears, or-- to bring-- to bring something home. And that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said-- you used the word 'ghetto' now, but at this point, was Austrazetz a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at this point, what they did, they brought another town by the name of Truvets. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god. T-R-U-V-E-T-Z, I think, is the closest I can come to it. And this shtetl was a little bigger than ours. I would take a guess, probably about 1,000 people. And then they fenced off half of the town and they put us all on this--

speaker: interviewer
text: So at my house, if I can remember correctly, there must have been probably between 30 and 40 people. I mean, because we had to. They took half of the town and brought in another town. It was really-- I mean, we used our barn, which we had for people to live. It was a real-- a real ghetto. And you couldn't-- it was fenced off. What was the fence made out of? Do you remember what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plain-- plain, you know, wire with the-- sticky wire. It wasn't the electric wire. It was not electrified.

speaker: interviewer
text: The barbed--

speaker: interviewee
text: The barbed wire. And in and out, you have to have a passport. We have to have the not a passport, but a [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] permission, you know? So some things were permitted to-- to come in and out. And also, at the same time, they-- they they gave us the yellow-- the yellow Magen David to wear. So as to-- you could not walk around without it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was when it-- the town-- the town became a ghetto and was fenced off. Do you recall what month in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it-- it's either very late '41 or very early '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the weather was like? Maybe that would help you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- I-- I-- I-- I believe it was-- it might have been in '41, because it was-- there was still no snow on the ground, and usually we get snow, well, we got snow very early there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms were in your house that it could accommodate 30 people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we-- we probably had a house of-- of seven rooms, you know? Well, because-- well, it's-- and then we had a barn. At that time, we had no horses or cows, so we converted that. And we-- we really slept four or five people per room, you know? And it's-- and we were the lucky ones because we had a lot of room. And we were the ones that our house was in a ghetto. We didn't have to move by coincidence, because the people that had to move, they were lucky to just get a space to sleep. I mean, it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: The people that came in with you-- had you known them before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well first, we had to get the whole family in. So all the five sisters and their kids. And probably-- and-- to bring them in. Then we had to do my father's side of the family, so which they were four and their-- and their kids. And we-- we had some distant relatives that we packed them in. And it was allocated, really. That-- that was allocated by the-- by the people that-- that ran the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: They more or less told you who had to--

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less yeah. Well, you-- you would said that you would have to-- you have to have so many people. Obviously, you put your relatives in there first. But after that, you know, they just make you-- made you get the quota. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
